1. **Pot Odds**: 3:1 is favorable. I need roughly 25% equity to breakeven, and A3o likely has approximately 30-35% equity against Flash's wide continuing range (he raises frequently from button/SB and will c-bet with many holdings).

2. **Opponent Profile**: Flash is very aggressive and raises wide. My ace-high has reasonable equity against a polarized range that includes many unpaired holdings, draws, and weaker aces.

3. **Flexibility**: Calling keeps the pot manageable (1200 before turn action) and allows me to reassess on the turn. If I catch an ace, I'll have value. If the turn is blank, I can fold to further aggression while minimizing losses.

4. **Stack Depth**: Both stacks are deep (me 16400, Flash 22700), so I'm not committed. I retain fold equity and pot control.

This is standard BB defending strategy - maintaining the fight with proper odds while playing the turn intelligently.

The turn brings an 8c to a Jd-8h-7c board, pairing the board. I have A3o (ace-high) with minimal hand strength on this coordinated board. The eight doesn't improve my hand and actually could complete straight draws for Flash's range (T9, 99-QQ are now more concerned, and TT/99/QQ/KK are likely in his continuing range).

Key considerations:
1. **Hand Strength**: A3o is now just ace-high on a paired board where Flash's continuing range includes many hands that beat me or have significant equity (overpairs, sets, straights like T9).
2. **Board Texture**: The paired eight makes this board less favorable for my ace. Flash likely has broadway cards, overpairs, or holdings that connected with the 7 or J.
3. **Pot Control**: Checking maintains flexibility. If Flash bets, I can reassess with new information and fold if the bet is substantial. If Flash checks, I check through and get to river cheaply.
4. **Stack Depth**: I still have good stacks (16100), so I don't need to force equity with a marginal hand.
5. **Opponent Tendencies**: Flash is aggressive but will also check some turns. Checking allows me to induce bluffs or see the river fairly inexpensively.

I'll check to maintain pot control, minimize losses with a weak hand, and retain flexibility on the river.

I have improved to trips (three eights) on the turn. This is a very strong hand, though the board is somewhat coordinated with potential straights (T9) and full houses (JJ, 77, J8, 87). Claude checked to me again, and I should bet for value and protection. A bet of 800 into the 1200 pot (roughly 66%) targets value from any Jack, a Seven, or various draws (flush draws or straight draws) that Claude might be holding.

Folding A3o on the J♦8♥7♣8♣ board after Flash's turn raise. While defending BB with A3o was reasonable and calling the flop aggression made sense with pot odds, the turn pairing is unfavorable and Flash's raise indicates strength. My hand has marginal equity (~20%) against his range, barely meeting the 3.5:1 odds required. The turn card potentially completed straight draws and helped his overpairs/sets. Calling commits more chips into a weak hand where I'll likely fold on the river against further aggression. Folding preserves my stack for better spots and is the disciplined play with borderline equity and unfavorable board texture.

7s3s is too weak to play from the button even heads-up. The hand has poor playability, limited equity, and doesn't warrant risking additional chips. Flash has shown consistent aggression, making this an easy fold. Preserving chips for stronger hands is the correct long-term strategy.

I am on the button with J6o. In a heads-up situation, J6o is a marginal hand but generally worth a raise or at least a completion. Given my significant chip lead (23,900 vs 15,950), I can afford to apply pressure or play small pots. However, J6o is weak enough that I don't want to build a massive pot unnecessarily. I will raise to 300 to try and take the blinds or play the hand with the initiative.

K4o is too weak to defend out of position against Flash's aggressive button raise. Despite reasonable pot odds (~3.3:1), the hand has limited upside, I lack positional advantage, and I'm vulnerable even if I hit the flop. Folding preserves stack equity for better opportunities.

2-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker - essentially the worst holding possible. While heads-up play from the button typically involves raising a wide range of hands, 2-3o is one of the few holdings that's fundamentally unplayable even with positional advantage. I'm currently slightly short-stacked against Flash (15900 vs 23950), so I need to preserve chips for better spots. The cost to call is only 50 more chips, but investing this hand would be -EV long-term. I should fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity when the blinds return to me with a playable holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-5 offsuit. While it is a heads-up situation where I should be aggressive from the button, 7-5 offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop. Given that Claude has been calling raises frequently and playing back at me, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Raising with KK to 300 total chips (3x BB). This premium hand demands aggressive play to build the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. The raise extracts value while maintaining a strong positional advantage heads-up.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit (AJs) in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Claude raised from the Button/Small Blind to 300. I have two main options: calling or 3-betting. Given the strength of the hand and the fact that I'm out of position, raising helps define the hand and take control of the pot. A 3-bet to 900-1000 is standard here. Since I want to maximize value and potentially end the hand pre-flop or build a pot with a superior range, I will raise.

Re-raising with pocket Kings in heads-up play. KK is a premium hand with strong equity against Flash's re-raising range. A 3-bet to 3,000 total applies appropriate pressure, builds the pot for value, and puts Flash in a difficult decision spot. This bet size is assertive while maintaining balanced play - strong enough to assert dominance but not so large as to commit too much equity too early in the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit. I initially 3-bet to 1,000 after Claude's button raise to 300. Claude has now 4-bet to 3,000. While AJ is a strong hand heads-up, facing a 4-bet from a competent player often means I am dominated by hands like AK, AQ, or high pocket pairs. Calling 2,000 more out of position would create a bloated pot with a low stack-to-pot ratio, making the post-flop play extremely difficult when I miss or hit marginal strength. Given my significant chip lead, I prefer to avoid this high-variance situation where I am likely behind his range.

I have pocket Kings, which is the second strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. In a heads-up situation, this is an extremely powerful hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot for value and to take initiative in the hand.

Calling with Ts-Jh from the big blind in heads-up play. This hand has decent playability with straight potential and reasonable post-flop equity against Flash's wide range. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds makes this a standard call. The hand plays well post-flop and allows me to realize equity against an aggressive opponent.

Checking with Ts-Jh on a 2h-Qs-Ad flop. This hand has an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) with decent equity (~40-45%). Checking allows me to pot control with a drawing hand, see another card for free, and keep the option to apply pressure later if the turn improves my hand. Flash's check suggests weakness, making a check-back appropriate to realize equity efficiently against a wide range in heads-up play.

I have pocket Kings, which is a very strong hand, but the flop has come Ace-high. This is a common situation where the pre-flop aggressor (me) might be expected to have an Ace. Since Claude checked, I will put out a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to represent the Ace and potentially get value from a Queen or a draw. If I am called, I will likely check the turn to control the pot size unless the board improves my hand.

Calling with Ts-Jh on 2h-Qs-Ad. I have an open-ended straight draw with 8 outs (any K or 9 completes my straight). With two cards to come, this gives me approximately 45% equity. Pot odds are 4:1 (calling 300 to win 1200), which means I need only ~20% equity to break even. My actual equity of 45% makes this a mathematically strong call. In heads-up play, Flash's flop bet to 300 after my check is consistent with continuation betting from a wide range. I'll call here to realize my draw equity, and I'm in good shape for the turn. If I hit my straight, I'll have a strong made hand. If I miss, I can reassess based on turn action and remaining stack sizes.

Checking on the turn with Ts-Jh on a 2h-Qs-Ad-2c board. I hold an 8-out open-ended straight draw with solid equity (~40-45%). Flash checked, indicating either weakness or a cautious hand. Checking back is the optimal play here in heads-up situations with a drawing hand—it allows me to realize my equity efficiently against a wide range while avoiding overcommitment. I have decent pot odds implied on the river (if Flash bets) and outs to make the strongest possible hand. This is a standard pot control approach that keeps my opponent's range wide and allows me to make informed decisions on the river based on Flash's final action.

On the turn, a second deuce has paired the board. Claude has checked to me after calling my continuation bet on the Ace-high flop. My pocket Kings are currently a strong bluff-catcher or value hand against a Queen, but they are behind any Ace or any Deuce. By checking back the turn, I control the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff on the river or get to a cheap showdown. Betting again might only get called by hands that beat me (like an Ace).

River decision on 2h-Qs-Ad-2c-5c with Ts-Jh. My open-ended straight draw completely missed—I needed a K or 9 and hit neither. My current hand is just Ace-Queen-high (Ad-Qs-Jh-Ts-5c), which is a weak holding with minimal showdown value. Flash checked the turn, suggesting either a weak/moderate hand or pot control with marginal strength. Given I have no equity-building opportunities remaining, betting would be a pure bluff with poor risk/reward—my hand will perform better as a check-down to showdown against Flash's likely range of Ace-combinations, pairs, or high-card hands. Checking also keeps the line consistent with my previous pot-control approach and allows Flash to either check behind (giving me showdown at no cost) or bet, at which point I can make a river-specific decision. This is the prudent play with a complete miss.

I have pocket Kings on an Ace-high board. Claude called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, but has checked the turn and river. While my hand is very strong in a vacuum, on this specific board it is essentially a bluff-catcher or a thin value hand. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by many hands I beat (perhaps only a Queen), while most hands that call me will be Aces that were playing cautiously or slow-played. By checking back, I ensure a showdown and realize my equity against Claude's range, which includes many Qx hands and missed draws that I currently beat.

Calling with K♥3♣ from the button in heads-up play. The 3:1 pot odds are favorable, and I maintain positional advantage post-flop against Flash's aggressive tendencies. This allows me to play optimally with information, folding easily to strong action on poor flops while capitalizing on pin-point aggression with position when the board improves my hand.

I have Ad Qd in the big blind, which is a very strong hand. Claude limped from the button, which often signifies a weaker or speculative range. I will raise to build the pot and take the lead with my premium holding.

Calling 300 more (400 total) with K♥3♣ from the button in heads-up play. The 2.6:1 pot odds are favorable, and I maintain positional advantage postflop against Flash's aggressive tendencies. This allows me to play optimally with information, folding easily to strong action on poor flops while capitalizing on well-placed aggression when the board improves my hand or when Flash overplays marginal holdings.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a strong hand (Ad Qd) and have missed the flop of 9d-4h-5c. However, as the pre-flop raiser, I maintain a range advantage over Claude, who limped and called. I will place a continuation bet of 400 to represent a strong hand and attempt to take the pot down now, while still having two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw if called.

Calling 400 more with K♥3♣ on a 9♦4♥5♣ flop facing a 400 bet into 1200. The 3:1 pot odds are excellent - I only need ~25% equity to break even. With king high (likely the best hand against many of Flash's continuation bets) plus a gutshot straight draw, I have substantial equity. Position remains valuable for gathering information on the turn. Flash's aggressive tendencies throughout the session suggest he's betting a wide range here.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more coordinated with the 6s. This card potentially completes straights (like 78) and improves many of the middle-to-low hands Claude might have called with on the flop, such as 76, 56, or 45. My Ace-Queen high still has some showdown value against his total bluffs or smaller Ace-highs, but a second barrel here is unlikely to push him off a pair given how well this runout connects with a button-limping range. I will check to see a river for free and re-evaluate if Claude bets, maintaining pot control on a dangerous board.

Flash checked the turn after betting the flop, indicating weakness. I'm likely ahead with king high and also hold a gutshot straight draw (any 7 or 2). By checking, I get a free river card to potentially improve while keeping the pot small and avoiding unnecessary confrontation. This allows me to maximize the value of my draw and positional advantage in heads-up play.

On the river, the board has paired with a second six, and the overall texture (9-4-5-6-6) is highly coordinated, completing several straights (78, 23) and improving hands like 76 or 86 to trips. Given that Claude limped from the button and called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet, his range is heavily weighted towards these middle-to-low cards. My Ace-Queen high has some showdown value against missed draws or weaker Ace-highs, but it is not strong enough to value bet, and turning it into a bluff is risky given how well this runout connects with a limping range. I will check and hope to reach showdown cheaply, likely folding if faced with a significant bet.

Betting 550 on the river after Flash checked twice. Flash's line (flop bet → turn check → river check) indicates weakness. With king high and positional advantage, I'm likely ahead of his range which likely contains missed draws and air. A 550 bet (roughly 1/3 pot) is sized to get called by weaker holdings while maximizing value when ahead. This extracts the most value from a weak hand while maintaining good risk/reward in a heads-up cash game context.

On this highly coordinated board of 9-4-5-6-6, my Ace-Queen high is extremely vulnerable. Claude's range as a button-limper is heavily weighted toward the middle and low cards that connect with this board, including potential straights, sets, or two-pairs that improved on the river. While his bet of 550 into a 1600 pot offers good odds (roughly 4:1), it is difficult to find many hands that he would bluff with here that I actually beat, as even his lowest pairs (like 22, 33, or any piece of the board) are currently ahead of me. Given the board texture and the likelihood that he has at least a small pair or better, I will fold and preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) is a strong hand from the button/small blind. I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand and a hand that is well ahead of an average random hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the action.

I'm in the BB with Kd-Js against Flash's button raise. This is a decent broadway hand that merits seeing a flop in heads-up play. The pot odds are favorable (400 in pot, 200 to call = 2:1). Flash has been active throughout the session but not excessively loose. Calling allows me to:

1) Keep the pot manageable relative to my 17050-chip stack
2) Play postflop with position advantage (I'll be out of position, but can play positionally relative to the flop texture)
3) Realize my hand's equity - KJ has decent playability and can hit strong on the flop
4) Avoid being overly aggressive with a marginal hand through 3-betting

Folding KJ to a single raise from the button is too passive given the situation and stack dynamics. This is a straightforward call.

I have top pair (Jacks) with King kicker on a dry flop (Jd-Ts-3s). Flash checked to me, indicating likely weakness. With strong hand equity against his button-raise range, I should bet for value. A 400-chip bet (roughly 2/3 pot) is standard sizing to build the pot, extract value from worse hands, and get information about his holding while maintaining good stack-to-pot ratios for future decisions.

The flop Jd-Ts-3s is quite connected and likely hits Claude's pre-flop calling range. Claude's decision to lead out (donk bet) for 400 into 600 is a strong signal of either a made hand (like a Jack or a Ten) or a significant draw (spades or a straight draw). Holding only Ace-high with no backdoors or draws on this texture, it is unprofitable to call. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Calling with 8-J offsuit on the button in heads-up against the big blind. Getting favorable 3:1 odds (150 to call 50) and maintaining positional advantage for postflop play. Jack is a decent card that plays well in position, and the button/small blind should play a wide range in heads-up poker. Stack sizes are healthy for flexible play.

I have 6-3 offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind has just completed the blind (called the 100 total). There is no incentive to raise with such a weak hand out of position, and I am entitled to see the flop for free by checking. I will check and evaluate the flop.

On this flop of 9c-7h-6h, I have bottom pair (sixes) with a weak kicker (3). The pot is small (200 chips) and I am in the Big Blind. My opponent (Claude) just called pre-flop. I have a piece of the board, but it is a very coordinated board with many straight and flush possibilities. Since I am first to act and the pot is small, checking is the most standard play to control the pot and see what my opponent does. I will check.

Betting 125 chips as a continuation bet on the flop. With J-8 offsuit on 9-7-6 board, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to make a straight) plus overcard potential. Flash's check from the big blind suggests weakness. In heads-up poker with position, aggression is profitable. This bet size is standard for c-betting the pot while maintaining good stack depth. I can win immediately if Flash folds, or continue with decent equity if called.

On a board of 9c-7h-6h, I have bottom pair (sixes) with a weak kicker. Claude's bet of 125 into a 200 pot represents a standard sizing on a coordinated board. While my hand is weak, folding bottom pair to a single bet in an unraised pot is generally too tight, especially in a heads-up situation where the opponent's range for completing the small blind and betting a wet flop is very wide. I will call to see the turn and evaluate based on the next card and Claude's subsequent action.

On a board of 9-7-6-10, my bottom pair of sixes is extremely weak and vulnerable. The ten is a very bad card for my range as it completes several straights (like 8-J) and improves many of the hands Claude would have bet on the flop. Since I am out of position and have very little showdown value, I will check to see how Claude proceeds. If he bets again, I will likely have to fold given how coordinated the board has become.

Checking back on the turn with my open-ended straight draw. Flash's check indicates weakness, and while I'm currently behind with J-high, my 8 outs provide solid equity to the river. Taking a free card is optimal here - if a K or 5 completes my straight, I have a strong hand. If I don't improve, I can play the river based on Flash's action. This is disciplined play that balances aggression with hand strength.

On a final board of 9c-7h-6h-Td-4d, I hold bottom pair (sixes). The turn checked through after Claude bet the flop and I called. This suggests Claude may not have a powerhouse like a straight, but he could easily hold a better pair (7, 9, or 10). My hand has marginal showdown value against missed draws like hearts or non-pairing overcards. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown; betting doesn't accomplish much as better hands will call and worse hands will fold.

River value bet with a made 10-high straight. Flash checked to me, indicating a weak hand. With a strong made hand and positional advantage, betting for value is standard. Sizing of 250 into a 450 pot is reasonable - aggressive enough to build the pot, not so large as to blow Flash off weaker hands that might call (pairs, busted draws, etc.).

On a board of 9-7-6-10-4, my bottom pair of sixes is incredibly weak. The board is highly coordinated, with any 8 making a straight. Claude's line—betting the flop, checking the turn, and then betting the river—could represent a marginal made hand like a seven or nine that checked the turn for pot control on the scare-card ten, or it could be a missed draw. However, even many of Claude's missed draws (like KhJh or QhJh) consist of overcards that have significant equity or have simply bluffed. While the pot odds are roughly 3:1, my hand is near the very bottom of my range here and loses to almost any 'value' hand Claude might hold. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 7c 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have a significant chip lead, Claude has been defending his big blind and playing aggressively post-flop in recent hands. There is little reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand when out of position post-flop if called. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Raising on the button in heads-up against Flash. T6o is playable given positional advantage, and a minimum raise to 200 applies pressure while allowing me to play in position postflop if called. This is standard button strategy in 2-player situations.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised from the Small Blind to 200. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my current chip lead and the poor quality of the hand, there is no reason to defend. I will fold.

I am on the Button with 2h 3d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even in a heads-up situation where I should play a wide range of hands, this specific hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the hand's lack of potential, folding is the most prudent play to avoid losing more chips in a marginal spot.

Calling the 50 to see the flop. Position on the button in heads-up play is valuable, and T5o has reasonable playability in this spot. The minimal cost to call (just 50 more chips) justifies seeing a flop where I can leverage positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding K3o. Claude, on the Button, has just limped in. K3o is a decent enough hand to see a flop with in a heads-up situation, especially when I can check back for free. There is no need to bloat the pot with a weak King out of position against a player who has shown they can be aggressive post-flop. I will check and take a look at the flop.

I have flopped top pair with a King. The board is relatively dry (Kd-4h-5c). In a limped pot, my goal is to extract value from Claude's weaker pairs, straight draws (like 67 or 32), or high-card hands that might feel the need to float. Since I am out of position, I will start by checking to see if Claude wants to bet his air or marginal hands, but betting for protection and value is also a strong option. Given the pot is small (200), a lead of 100-150 can help define the hand. However, checking allows Claude to bluff. Let's try a small lead to see where we stand and protect against overcards or small draws.

Calling Flash's min-raise on the flop with a pair of fives. The 3:1 pot odds justify a call with a made hand, and position advantage allows me to control the hand post-flop. Flash's min-raise could represent various holdings, and calling allows me to gather more information on the turn before making further decisions.

On the turn, the 3rd card of diamonds arrives, but more importantly, it pairs my 3, giving me two pair (Kings and Threes). This is a significant improvement. The board is now Kd-4h-5c-3d. I led out for 100 on the flop and Claude called, suggesting he has a piece of the board or a draw. I want to continue betting for value and to protect against straight draws (like 67 or A2) and the backdoor diamond draw. A bet of 300 into the 400 pot is a strong value bet that charges his draws and weaker made hands.

With a pair of fives and Ten kicker on a Kd 4h 5c 3d board, I'm facing Flash's turn raise to 300. Analyzing the situation: (1) Flash's action - raising on the turn after checking the flop suggests a strong holding, likely a King, higher pair, or strong draw that hit. (2) My equity - Against Flash's likely range (KK, KQ, KJ, KJ, AK, AQ, or other Broadway cards), my pair of fives is probably only 20-25% equity. (3) Pot odds required - I need to call 300 to win a pot of 700, requiring ~30% equity to break even. (4) My hand strength ranking - A pair of fives is marginal at best in this spot and likely dominated. This is a disciplinary fold of a hand that's probably beat and doesn't justify the risk given the improved pot odds are tight. Better to preserve my stack for stronger opportunities.

I am on the button with T3o. This is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While I have been aggressive in previous hands, T3o doesn't have much post-flop playability. However, given the stack sizes and the 150-hand limit, I can occasionally fold or call to see a cheap flop, but folding is the standard play with such a weak hand to avoid unnecessary losses. I will fold.

Heads-up on the button with K2o. While K2 is a weak hand, position is critical in heads-up play. With only 50 chips to call into a 150-chip pot, I should exploit button advantage and raise. A min raise to 200 accomplishes several things: (1) establishes aggression in position, (2) gives Flash weak holdings a reason to fold, (3) builds the pot when I have post-flop positioning advantage. K2 is marginal at best, but paired with button position and reasonable stack depths (I have 17,675, Flash has 22,175), this is a standard play. If Flash calls, I can control the action post-flop with my position advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o. Claude has raised to 200 from the Button (Small Blind). The pot is 300, and it costs me 100 more to call. J7o is a marginal hand, but getting 3:1 pot odds in a heads-up situation, it's a standard call to see a flop. Claude has been active and aggressive, and I have a significant stack lead. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate my position then.

On this flop of 2c-Ks-2d, I have Jack-high with no direct draw. This board is quite static. My opponent, Claude, raised pre-flop and as the aggressor, a King is well within his range. However, this paired board also makes it likely that neither of us has connected strongly. Since I am in the Big Blind and the pot is 400 with no bet currently facing me, I will check. My goal is to see a turn cheaply or potentially catch a bluff if Claude bets small, although folding to significant pressure is likely the plan given my weak hand.

Two pair on the flop is a premium hand. Flash checked a paired, coordinated flop after calling my pre-flop raise, suggesting weakness. I'm betting 250 (5/8 pot) to extract value from weaker hands while maintaining my aggressive pre-flop line. This bet size gets paid by hands I beat (medium pairs, A-high) while building the pot when ahead. Position and hand strength both favor aggression here.

Claude has made a continuation bet of 250 into a 400 pot on a paired, dry board (2c-Ks-2d). My Jack-high has very little equity against his range, which contains many Kings and pocket pairs. While I have the chip lead, there's no reason to contest this pot with J-high on a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range more than mine. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jd 7d, a suited hand with some playability in a heads-up situation. The pot is currently 150 with a small 50-chip call to me. Claude has been active and sometimes aggressive, but with a stack of over 22,000 against his 17,825, I should look to apply pressure or play pots in position. Raising here is a standard play to take control of the hand pre-flop and potentially win the blinds or set up a post-flop continuation bet. I'll raise to 300.

Flash raised to 300 from the button with a relatively small raise size (3x). I hold J8o in the big blind, which is a weak hand that doesn't warrant defending here. J-high with poor kicker is below my standard defend range for BB against button raises, especially in the late stage of the tournament (hand 130/150). The 200 chips to call represents a significant cost relative to the marginal equity this hand has. With only 20 hands remaining and adequate stack depth, I should preserve chips by folding weak holdings and waiting for better spots.

Folding J-5o from the small blind. While J-5 becomes more playable in heads-up play, it's still a weak hand that falls below my standard small blind range. Against Flash's aggressive tendencies, J-5o has limited equity even with position. The hand has poor connectivity, weak kicker, and would be difficult to play post-flop. With 17,775 chips remaining and 19 hands left to play, it's better to preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings. The modest 3:1 pot odds don't justify calling with a hand that likely has less than 25% equity against Flash's big blind range.

In Hand #132, I am on the button with 8s 9d. In heads-up play, 89o is a strong enough hand to raise from the button (Small Blind) for value and to put pressure on the Big Blind. Raising to 300 has been a standard opening for me throughout the session, and it remains a solid play here to take control of the pot early.

Folding 4s-Jc from the big blind. This is a weak, disconnected hand (J4o) that lacks the strength needed to defend profitably against Flash's raise from the button. Even though I'm getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 to win 400), hand strength matters more than pot odds in this spot. I'll be out of position post-flop with a vulnerable hand that has poor equity against Flash's typical raising range. With 18-19 hands remaining and a slightly shorter stack (17675 vs 21925), I should be selective and wait for stronger holdings to contest raised pots.

8-3 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with insufficient equity (under 25%) to justify calling even at favorable pot odds of 3:1. With 17 hands remaining and a slightly shorter stack, chip preservation is important. This is a standard fold.

I am on the button with 9s5s, which is a decent suited connector/gapper in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly larger than Claude's. Opening from the button (small blind) is a standard aggressive play to apply pressure, though since I am currently 'to call' only 50 to complete the blind or raise, I will opt for a standard 3x raise to 300. This maintains my aggressive table image and puts the decision on the big blind.

I'm in the big blind with 8d-Qs against Flash's raise to 300 from the button. In heads-up play, defending the big blind is crucial to avoid constant blind erosion. While 8-Q offsuit is marginal, I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call with 400 in the pot), which requires only ~33% equity to break even. Flash has been aggressive throughout and likely has a wide range here. This hand has reasonable playability with overcards and can improve on the flop. Playing it post-flop with position disadvantage but reasonable equity makes sense compared to folding passively.

Checking with 8d-Qs on Ac-Th-Kh. This is essentially air with no equity - no pair, no draw, just two queen kickers against Flash's likely coordinated range. Out of position and facing a dangerous board, checking is correct. I'll assess Flash's action and play from there. Betting or raising would be overplaying a weak holding in bad position.

The flop of Ac-Th-Kh is extremely heavy for any calling range and connects very well with broadway cards. I have Nine-high with no direct draw or pair. Claude checked to me. While I could attempt a continuation bet, this board is so favorable for a typical defending range that includes hands like Ax, Kx, QJ, or even J9 that a bluff is unlikely to succeed if Claude has any piece of it. I will check behind to see a free turn card and evaluate if I pick up a spade draw or some other equity.

Turn card is 7h, bringing the board to Ac-Th-Kh-7h. I have 8d-Qs, which is complete air - no pair, no draw, no equity. Flash checked the flop behind, suggesting weakness or a middling hand. 

With no made hand and no draw possibilities (the 7h doesn't give me straight or flush draw opportunities), I have no value to bet. Out of position and with a whiffed hand, checking is the only reasonable option. This allows the hand to progress to the river where I can make a final decision based on Flash's action and the river card. Betting would be overplaying a zero-equity hand in bad position against an opponent who may have any piece of this coordinated board.

On the turn, the Seven of Hearts brings a possible flush to the board (if Claude has hearts). My hand remains 9-high with no pair and no draw. Claude has checked twice now (on the flop and turn). While this might look like weakness, the board (Ac Th Kh 7h) is incredibly dangerous and likely to have connected with a large portion of Claude's big blind defense range. Since I have zero equity and there are many cards in Claude's range that he won't fold on this board, I will check back to see a free river card.

Betting 200 on the river with a pair of queens (8d-Qs on Ac-Th-Kh-7h-Qd). Flash's passive line—checking flop, turn, and river—indicates a weak holding or missed draw. My pair of queens with ace-king-ten kickers is a strong hand here. A 1/3 pot bet extracts value from worse holdings (lower pairs, ace-queen, missed draws) while avoiding overcommitting with a hand that still can lose to better two pairs or sets Flash might be slow-playing. The aggressive nature of this game suggests Flash may call with marginal hands, making this a profitable value bet.

The board has completed a straight (any Jack) and there are three hearts present. My hand is 9-high with no showdown value. Claude bet 200 into a 600 pot. While the bet is small, the board is extremely connected and likely hit a large portion of Claude's defending range. I checked the flop and turn, showing no strength. Attempting a bluff here against a board that offers so many pairs, straights, and flushes is too risky. I will fold and move to the next hand.

I'm on the button (small blind) with KJ offsuit, a premium hand. Stack depth is reasonable at 179 big blinds. Flash is playing an aggressive style with standard 300 raises, but I should control the action early with aggression from position. KJ has strong equity and benefits from position-based advantage postflop. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate here - it applies pressure, establishes my aggressive range, and allows me to continue profitably on most flops given my position and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five suited (Ah5h), a strong hand in a heads-up or small-table scenario. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. Ah5h is an excellent candidate for a 3-bet, as it has good equity against many of Claude's opening hands and possesses great playability post-flop with nut-flush potential and straight possibilities. By raising now, I can potentially win the pot immediately or build a larger pot for when I hit the board.

Calling Flash's 3-bet with KJ offsuit from the button. Analysis: (1) Hand strength is decent - KJ has solid equity against Flash's wide opening range; (2) Pot odds are favorable at 2.5:1 (need to call 500 into 1000); (3) Position is critical - I'll act last post-flop and can leverage this advantage to win pots and control the action; (4) Stack depth is reasonable at ~177 big blinds - I have room to evaluate the flop and make informed decisions without being committed; (5) Opponent profile - Flash has been playing aggressively throughout the session with frequent raises and c-bets, suggesting a wide range rather than premium holdings; (6) A fold is unnecessarily passive here; raising risks building a large pot out of position if Flash has a strong hand. The optimal play is to call, realize position equity, and play flops effectively.

On a King-high board (Kd 7d 4s) after being the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a significant range advantage. My 3-betting range heavily includes strong Kings like AK and KQ, as well as AA. Claude's range for calling a 3-bet is often capped, consisting of middle pairs or suited connectors. I will place a continuation bet of approximately one-third of the pot (500) to maintain initiative, represent the King, and potentially fold out middle pairs or overcards that have missed the board.

Calling with top pair and a good kicker (Kh Js on 7d 4s Kd board). Flash c-bets 500 into 2000 pot after 3-betting pre-flop. Pot odds are 3.5:1, highly favorable. Position is critical - I act last on remaining streets and can control the action. Stack depth is healthy at 17175. Against Flash's wide 3-bet range, KJ has strong equity. Calling keeps the pot manageable while realizing position advantage to win additional pots or thin-value bet on future streets. Raising risks building a large pot out of position when Flash may have a premium hand; folding is unnecessarily passive with top pair and good kicker.

The turn Eight of Spades is a relatively neutral card. Having 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the King-high flop, I have represented a strong range. However, Claude called the flop bet, which indicates he likely has a piece of the board, such as a King, a Seven, or a draw. My Ace-Five of Hearts currently only has Ace-high value and limited equity to improve. Since Claude's range is likely to contain several hands that won't fold to a second barrel (like KJ, KQ, or even Kx suited), I will check to control the pot size and see how he responds. This allows me to potentially realize my equity if the river is an Ace or to catch him if he is also on a draw.

Betting 700 on the turn with top pair and Jack kicker. Flash's check indicates weakness—likely a medium-strength hand that doesn't want further commitment. This moderate bet (roughly 1/4-1/3 pot) extracts value from weaker holdings (AQ, underpairs, whiffed draws) while respecting stack depths. Position advantage is critical: I'll act last on the river and can evaluate Flash's response with perfect information. If Flash had a strong hand, a continuation bet was more likely on the turn. This betting line is consistent with my aggressive pre-flop narrative and exploits Flash's apparent weakness while maintaining a manageable pot.

Claude called my pre-flop 3-bet and my flop continuation bet on a King-high board, which suggests he has a piece of the board, likely a King or a middle pair. My check on the turn signaled weakness, and Claude's small bet of 700 into a 2500 pot (less than 1/3 pot) could be a value bet or a probe bet. However, my Ace-Five of Hearts is only Ace-high with no draws and only has about three outs (the remaining Aces) to improve to the best hand. Given the board texture and Claude's persistence, it's highly probable he has a better hand or a draw with significant equity. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with T7o. This is a marginal hand, but in a heads-up situation, I want to maintain a high level of aggression to keep my opponent on their toes and win pots pre-flop. A standard open to 3bb (300 total) is appropriate to apply pressure from the button.

Standard big blind defense. J9 has reasonable draw potential with 2:1 pot odds against Flash's aggressive button opening range. Playing from the worst position requires caution post-flop, but defending blinds is standard strategy in 2-player dynamics.

Flash raised preflop from the button and I defended with J9 from the big blind with reasonable pot odds. On the K-3-8 flop, Flash has checked to me. My hand is currently weak with only a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to any 10). Given my poor position postflop and marginal hand, checking is the appropriate play. This allows me to: (1) control pot size without overcommitting with a weak holding, (2) see another card and potentially hit my draw or improve, and (3) avoid bloating the pot when my equity is limited. Against an aggressive opponent like Flash, I can re-evaluate on the turn with more information.

On the flop of Kd-3s-8s, I have Ten-high and no direct draw besides some very thin runner-runner possibilities. My opponent, Claude, has checked to me. This board favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor since I have more Kings in my range. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to try and take the pot down immediately, as I expect Claude to fold many of his misses here.

Calling Flash's flop bet with J9 on a K-3-8 board. My gutshot straight draw (4 outs) provides approximately 25-30% equity, which is close to breakeven at 3:1 pot odds. The call allows me to: (1) realize my equity on the draw, (2) take advantage of implied odds if I hit the Ten, (3) maintain flexibility on the turn with better information, and (4) avoid overcommitting out of position with a marginal hand. Raising is too aggressive given the hand strength, and folding surrenders too much equity.

Flash checked the turn after betting the flop, suggesting weakness. With J9 and a gutshot straight draw (4 outs, ~8.7% equity), I should take the free card to the river. Betting out of position with limited equity is not profitable; checking allows me to realize my equity while maintaining control. If I hit the Ten, I'll have a made hand. If I miss, I lose the minimum in a small pot.

The turn 4d doesn't change much on this board and I still have Ten-high. My continuation bet on the flop was called by Claude, which indicates he might have a piece of the board like an 8, a 3, or a flush draw. Since I have no equity and the turn didn't improve my hand or my range's perceived strength significantly enough to force a fold from a pair, checking back to take a free river and potentially see a cheap showdown (or give up) is the best play to control the pot size with zero showdown value.

River with J9 (no made hand). Flash checked the turn, indicating weakness. My J9 is pure air with no showdown value. Betting would be a bluff that's unlikely to succeed given Flash's weakness display. Checking allows me to take a free showdown and minimize losses in a small 1,200 pot. If Flash has a hand, he may value bet anyway. If he checks back, the hand goes to showdown where J9 will likely lose but without additional risk.

On this dry board, my Ten-high has zero showdown value. However, the river 6h is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. Claude has checked for the third time (flop, turn, and river). While this often signals weakness, he did call my continuation bet on the flop, which suggests he has a piece of the board (like an 8 or a 3) or a drawing hand that missed. Given that the turn went check-check, my range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor has diminished slightly. Betting here would be a pure bluff. I've decided to check back and concede the pot, as Claude's range likely contains enough marginal made hands (like weak pairs or Ace-high) that might call a river bet, and I'd prefer to minimize losses with a hand that has no equity.

I'm holding King-Ace (Kd-Ac) on the button, which is one of the premium starting hands in poker. With a stack of 19,725 and Flash having a similar stack of 20,125, we have deep enough stacks for multi-street play. 

KA is a strong hand that dominates most of Flash's opening range. By raising to 200 (minimum raise), I'm:
1. Playing a premium hand from excellent position
2. Building the pot with a strong hand
3. Putting pressure on Flash and gaining initiative
4. Maintaining fold equity while being able to see more cards if called

Flash has been an aggressive opponent who raises frequently, but KA is strong enough to play for value and protection. The button position gives me additional advantage post-flop.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Seven of diamonds, a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Claude has raised from the button/small blind to 200 total (a 2x raise). Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I am out of position, I have several options. While calling is standard to keep the pot controlled, three-betting (raising) for value and to take initiative is also a very viable strategy with suited Aces. However, seeing as Claude has been active, I will choose to three-bet to 600 to define the hand early and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control on the flop.

I'm holding King-Ace (Kd-Ac), one of the premium starting hands in poker. Flash has 3-bet my initial raise to 600, and I need to decide how to proceed.

Key factors supporting a 4-bet:
1. **Hand strength**: KA is among the top 1% of starting hands and performs well against Flash's 3-betting range
2. **Position**: I'm on the button, which is critical for post-flop play
3. **Stack depth**: With ~20k effective stacks, I have room for multi-street play and can comfortably build a pot with premium hands
4. **Opponent profile**: Flash has shown an aggressive 3-betting tendency throughout the session, suggesting a potentially wider range that KA dominates

A 4-bet re-raise to 1500 accomplishes several things:
- Applies meaningful pressure (2.5x Flash's bet) 
- Tests the strength of Flash's hand without overcommitting pre-flop
- Extracts value against medium pairs and hands like AQ that KA dominates
- Maintains my aggressive table image while backed by legitimate hand strength
- If Flash folds, I win the pot; if called, I'll play post-flop with position and a premium holding

This is a straightforward premium hand play in position against an aggressive opponent.

Claude has responded to my 3-bet with a 4-bet to 1500. While Ace-Seven suited is a strong hand in heads-up play, calling a 4-bet out of position creates a difficult post-flop scenario, especially since Claude's 4-betting range is likely to contain stronger Aces and pocket pairs. Given that I have already 3-bet, folding here prevents me from being trapped in a large pot with a dominated hand.

I am on the button with K7o, which is a standard open-raising hand in heads-up play. Claude has been relatively active, but a King-high hand holds significant equity and serves as a good candidate to apply pressure. I will raise to my standard 3x sizing (300 total) to take control of the hand pre-flop.

Folding 67o from the big blind against Flash's button raise. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Flash has shown consistent aggression on the button throughout this session, raising frequently. With 67o, I lack the hand strength to profitably engage in a raised pot, especially out of position. In heads-up play, while ranges widen, I should still maintain discipline against weak holdings that play poorly against aggressive opponents. The risk-reward does not justify calling here.

6h-4h (6-4 suited) in heads-up from the button is a standard call. I'm already 50 in (SB), getting 3:1 odds on a 150 chip pot to call 50. In heads-up poker, this hand has reasonable equity against Flash's wide range. I have position post-flop and can leverage that advantage. The pot odds justify a call with any reasonable hand, and 6-4 suited is definitely playable with straight and flush potential.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit (KsQc), which is a very strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. Claude has completed the small blind (limped). My hand is significantly ahead of a typical limping range. I will raise to 400 to build the pot and take the initiative, as my hand plays well both pre-flop and post-flop.

Standard call in heads-up from the button with 6-4 suited. Good pot odds (5:3), positional advantage post-flop, reasonable equity against Flash's wide range, and good playability with straight/flush potential. Must defend appropriately wide in heads-up to avoid exploitation.

I have flopped top pair with a king kicker on a board of 8s-Qd-7s. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. The board is somewhat wet with both straight and flush draws available (spades), so I want to bet for value and to deny equity to potential draws. Claude's pre-flop call of my raise suggests a range that includes middle pairs, Broadway cards, or suited connectors. I will bet 500 into the 800 pot to maintain the initiative and extract value from Qx, 8x, 7x, and draws.

Calling with 6-4 of hearts on 8s-Qd-7s. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) plus a flush draw (9 additional outs), giving approximately 30-35% equity. The pot odds of 2.6:1 are favorable for this drawing hand. Flash's flop bet is a standard continuation bet, and my position remains favorable post-turn. This is a profitable call with sufficient equity and no need to overcommit with a raise.

The turn 3h is a blank that doesn't complete the straight or flush draws present on the flop. My top pair, King kicker remains very strong in this heads-up pot. Claude called a significant bet on the flop, suggesting he likely has a pair or a draw. I will continue betting for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of 1000 into the 1800 pot is a standard sizing to keep the pressure on and extract value from weaker Queens and various draws.

6-4 of hearts on 8s-Qd-7s-3h turn board. I hold a powerful draw with 8 straight outs (5 or 9) plus 8 flush outs, totaling approximately 14 unique outs (30-32% equity). Pot odds of 2.8:1 require only 26% equity to break even, so this call is profitable. In heads-up poker, such strong draws with good pot odds and position are automatic calls. The variance is high but equity-positive, and if I hit one of my outs on the river, I win a significant pot.

On the river, the board is 8s-Qd-7s-3h-2d, and I hold KsQc for top pair with a King kicker. The river 2d is another total blank, meaning none of the obvious draws (spades or various straight draws) have completed. Given Claude has called my bets on both the flop and turn, his range likely consists of weaker Queens (QJ, QT, Q9), middle pairs (99, TT), or missed draws. Since I have the betting lead and Claude has played relatively sticky, I will make a value bet. A bet of 2000 into a 3800 pot (roughly 53% pot) is a standard sizing to extract final value from a weaker Queen while not overcommitting if he happened to slow-play a monster like a set or two-pair.

River fold with Q-high on 8s-Qd-7s-3h-2d. I was drawing with 6-4h on the flop and turn with good equity, but completely missed all my outs. With no made hand and only Q-high, I need roughly 26% equity to break even on the 2,000 call for a 7,800 pot. Q-high has minimal equity (5-10%) against Flash's range after he aggressively bet every street. Calling requires Flash to be bluffing far too frequently. Standard fold when draws miss and you have no showdown value.

I have Ace-Jack of hearts on the button, a very strong premium hand in a heads-up situation. My stack is significantly larger than Claude's, and I want to build the pot. A standard 3x open (to 300) is effective here to extract value and put pressure on the Big Blind. Based on previous hands, Claude often calls pre-flop raises but can be pushed off hands on later streets if the board is unfavorable.

Raising to 900 with pocket jacks. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively heads-up. The 3-bet applies appropriate pressure against Flash's button raise and either wins the pot immediately or gets value from a continuation. JJ is ahead of most of Flash's raising range and needs to build the pot in my favor.

I hold Ace-Jack of Hearts on the button in a heads-up match. This is a premium starting hand. I opened to 300, and Claude has three-bet (re-raised) to 900. Given the depth of stacks and the strength of my suited broadway hand, folding is out of the question. Calling allows me to play a pot in position with a strong hand, keeping his range wider and avoiding a massive pot pre-flop where I might be against a narrow range of AQ, AK, or big pairs if I were to four-bet. I will call the 600 more to see a flop with positional advantage.

Betting JJ on a 4h-3c-Kd flop. This is a premium overpair on a relatively dry flop that doesn't present obvious threats to my hand. The K on the board doesn't change the equity picture dramatically - I'm ahead of most of Flash's range except premium holdings (KK, AA, QQ, AK). By betting around half the pot (900), I build value with my strong hand, apply aggression in a heads-up spot, and gather information about Flash's holding. This is standard value betting with an overpair and maintains my aggressive table image.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack of hearts. I called a three-bet pre-flop, and the flop is King-High. Claude has fired a continuation bet of 900 into a 1800 pot (making it 2700). While I have overcards and a backdoor flush/straight draw, the King significantly hits a three-bettor's range (AK, KQ, KJs). However, Ace-High still has some showdown value against his potential bluffs or lower pocket pairs (like 99-JJ). Given the current stack sizes and my positional advantage, I don't want to give up immediately, but raising would likely only get called by better hands. Calling allows me to see how the turn develops. If he fires another large bet on the turn, I will likely have to fold unless I improve.

Betting JJ on the turn after Flash checks a paired board. My overpair is strong on this dry 4-3-K-4 runout. Flash's check indicates weakness despite calling the flop. A value bet of 1,800 (approximately half-pot) applies appropriate pressure, extracts value from hands like AQ, AJ, or Q-high, and keeps the pot manageable. This bet sizes maintains aggression while gathering information about Flash's holdings on the river.

On a board of 4h-3c-Kd-4d, I am holding Ace-high (Ah-Jh). Claude 3-bet pre-flop and has now fired two barrels, including a half-pot bet of 1800 on the turn. The board is now paired, which doesn't improve my hand. Given Claude's line, he is very likely to have a King (AK, KQ, KJs) or a strong pocket pair (AA, QQ, JJ, TT). While my Ace-high has some showdown value, it is likely behind his value range and I only have a few outs to improve to the best hand. Calling here would put a significant portion of my stack at risk on the river without a made hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Raising with A6s from the button in heads-up play. This is a strong hand in this context and warrants aggression from position. The min raise to 200 is a standard sizing that applies pressure while allowing me to see the flop if called. My recent aggressive strategy has been profitable, and A6s is well within my raising range.

I am in the big blind with 8h 3c. Claude, on the button/small blind, has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. 83 offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While the pot odds are 3:1, the post-flop playability of 83o is extremely poor, and I'll likely find myself folding to any continuation bet if I don't hit the flop hard. Given the late stage of the match (hand 141 of 150) and the relatively even stacks, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand.

In a heads-up situation, K7o is a solid hand to open from the button. I have been consistently raising to 300 chips throughout the match, and I will maintain that sizing to apply pressure on the big blind.

I'm in the Big Blind with 3h5h, one of the weakest hands in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. While the pot odds (400 to 200) are tempting on the surface, several factors make this a clear fold: (1) 3-5 is a marginal hand with limited postflop potential, (2) I'm out of position, which puts me at a significant disadvantage, (3) Flash has shown reasonable aggression and likely has a stronger range from the Button, (4) With deep stacks (~20k each), I don't need to play marginal holdings. This is exactly the type of hand to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities. I'm hand #142 of 150, so there are still many hands remaining to play better holdings.

2-9 offsuit is the worst hand in poker and is unplayable in almost all situations. While I'm on the button in heads-up play (best position), this hand has virtually no equity and poor playability. Folding weak hands and waiting for premium holdings has been a key part of my strategy throughout this session. With 20,125 chips and only 8 hands remaining, I can afford to be selective and fold marginal/terrible hands like this.

I am holding 7d 2s, the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the game nearing its conclusion (hand 144 of 150) and the stacks being nearly even, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with such low equity, especially against an opponent who has shown recent aggression. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity in the remaining few hands.

In heads-up play from the button (small blind) with Ac8h, this is a clear raise. Ace-high is a strong hand in heads-up poker and well-positioned for opening aggression. With nearly equal stacks and 6 hands remaining in the session, maintaining chip lead and playing positionally sound poker is important. A standard raise to 3x BB (300 total) is appropriate here to take down the pot or play in position postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Claude has raised to 3x the Big Blind from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, Q2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given that we are entering the final stage of the match (hand 145 of 150) and the stacks are nearly identical, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than bleed chips out of position with a weak range.

I am on the button with 7d 5s, which is a weak hand. Given that it's near the end of the match and the stacks are relatively even, I should maintain a strategy of folding marginal or weak hands to avoid unnecessary risks. 75o is not a hand I want to play out of position or even as a raise from the small blind in this heads-up scenario, especially as Claude has shown aggression in recent hands. Folding is the safest play here.

QQ is a premium starting hand in any format, and particularly valuable in heads-up play. I'm on the button with excellent position. A raise to 200 total (150 additional chips) builds the pot while showing strength. This allows me to play for value with one of the strongest possible starting hands, especially important with only 4 hands remaining in the match.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 4c, which is a very weak hand. Claude has opened for a min-raise (200). While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely difficult to play post-flop and rarely realizes its equity. Given we are near the end of the match (Hand 147 of 150) and I am slightly behind in chips, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to close the gap rather than bleed chips on marginal defenses.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with a pocket pair (4s 4c). In a heads-up situation, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and represent strength.

With only 3 hands remaining and a chip lead, folding 8-9 offsuit is prudent. The hand lacks sufficient strength to justify calling in this spot, and with limited opportunities left, I should wait for better spots to apply pressure or play premium hands. This preserves my chip advantage.

I'm on the button with 7d-Js in a heads-up situation. I need to complete 50 more chips to see the flop (already have 50 in as small blind). This is a reasonable hand with decent playability in position. With chip parity and two hands to go before the end, completing the small blind is standard. I have position postflop which gives me significant edge. The pot is already 150, and calling only 50 more means I'm getting proper odds to play. If I miss the flop, I can fold easily. If I connect, I can play aggressively from position.

With 8h 7c in the Big Blind and the Small Blind merely calling, I have the option to check and see a flop for free. Given the late stage of the game (Hand 149 of 150) and the nearly equal stacks, a check is a safe, low-variance play to potentially see a favorable board without inflating the pot unnecessarily.

I have bottom pair (sevens) on a board with two overcards (King and Queen) and a flush draw. In a limped pot, my goal is to reach a cheap showdown or improve. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (Kx, Qx, or draws), so checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and see how the opponent responds.

I flopped a pair of sevens with Jack kicker on a 7s-Qs-Kd board. Flash checked, suggesting weakness. With only two hands remaining and chip parity, I should value bet this hand. My pair is likely best currently but vulnerable to overcards on later streets, so I should build the pot now while ahead. A 125 chip bet represents a reasonable value sizing that charges draws and worse made hands while keeping the pot manageable.

I have bottom pair (sevens) on a board featuring a King and a Queen. In a limped pot, my hand has some value, and the bet of 125 into a 200 pot gives me decent odds to call and see a turn. Since we are near the end of the game and stacks are very close, I want to avoid inflating the pot with a marginal hand but should defend against a single small bet.

On the turn, the board has paired the Queen (7s Qs Kd Qh). I currently have one pair of sevens with an eight kicker. This board is quite heavy with overcards (King and Queen), and while the pairing of the Queen makes it slightly less likely my opponent holds one, any King or Queen still has me beat. In a limped pot with a marginal hand, my priority is to reach showdown as cheaply as possible. Checking here is the best way to control the pot and evaluate Claude's response.

With only 2 hands left and chip parity, every decision is critical. I have a pair of 7s with K-Q-J kickers. Flash's turn check signals weakness - they either missed their draw entirely or have a marginal holding. In heads-up poker with limited hands remaining, value betting from position against a checking opponent is standard. A ~250 chip bet (roughly 56% of the pot) applies appropriate pressure while extracting value from worse hands that might call or fold. This is my chance to gain chips before the final hand.

On the turn, the board has paired the Queen (7s-Qs-Kd-Qh), and I hold bottom pair with my sevens. While the pairing of the Queen makes it slightly less likely that Claude holds a Queen, he led on the flop and is now firing a second barrel on the turn. This board is very heavy with overcards (King and Queen) that easily hit a limped range. With only one hand remaining after this (Hand 150), and the stack sizes being quite close, I cannot justify calling a 250-chip bet with bottom pair on such a coordinated board. I will fold here to conserve my stack and look for a better opportunity to take the lead in the final hand where I will have the advantage of position.

As this is the final hand of the game and I am currently trailing Claude by 1050 chips, I am holding 4d 3h, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given the deficit and the lack of potential in this hand, the optimal play to protect my remaining stack and avoid unnecessary losses is to fold, as the probability of winning a large enough pot to overtake the lead with this hand is extremely low against a competent opponent.
